author: potuz
content: I don't want an alternative btw, I just like to remove the extra VC work

author: raulvk
content: haha, still, i want to make sure we explore more ideas and explicitly discard them

author: raulvk
content: aggregator-based solution are promising, but on surface leakier on the consensus, slashing, and networking fronts IMO. maybe we can all share some end shapes we're imagining, and walk backwards? e.g.:

author: raulvk
content: - cross-committee aggregates could skip the subnets and be submitted directly to the global topic. this could enable optimal packing efficiency for operators (they could send a single message for all their atts no matter what committees they were scheduled on). maybe the protocol can support the notion of "static aggregators". might synergize with rainbow staking and be useful for PQ sigs down the road

author: raulvk
content: ˆ^ a priori sounds complex and definitely not feasible for Hegotá, maybe worthwhile to continue exploring for future forks

author: raulvk
content: * reducing the number of subnets in this EIP would be the more pragmatic way to achieve *better* packing efficiency though

author: raulvk
content: * liberalizing the role of the aggregator: by itself doesn't improve efficiency, i view it more as a possible component of other solutions.

author: raulvk
content: * what if cross-aggregators published the seal ahead of time, which now acts like a binding promise that the attestations from those attesters can come only from this aggregator, and will appear directly on the global topic in aggregated form? this payload would act like a slashing input. downside: eliminates the fallback freedom validators have with EIP-8243 to publish a single att if their expected aggregate is not seen

author: serenita_io
content:
I think the "decoupled consensus" ideas should also be taken into account when considering this EIP ( initial core idea described here https://ethresear.ch/t/lmd-ghost-with-256-validators-and-a-fast-following-finality-gadget/22856 , another good intro article on this https://consensus.ethereum.foundation/blog/upgrading-finality-edition-1 ).

The outcome of that research will probably get us to only a few hundred validators casting a head vote every slot, which would arguably remove the need for this EIP, or at least significantly change it (we may still want to pre-aggregate source+target votes I suppose).

author: potuz
content: I think that's orthogonal to this EIP. The main benefit of this EIP is the reduction of the number of committees which will be still needed for finalization under decoupled finality

author: raulvk
content: <@737740104281489531> that’s only the fast available chain. For finality, you still need to hear from a supermajority of the active validator set. The decoupling detaches finality votes from block validity votes, so they flow separately allowing the former to degrade gracefully with network conditions. This EIP is useful in any network design where you want to hear from BLS identities managed by the same entity

author: serenita_io
content:
Just wanted to mention that research direction since I thought it to be relevant context.

Once we're only talking about finality votes, slot time is no longer a constraint. In that world we could have fewer committees on a longer –epoch– level which could open up the design space.

author: .paulharris
content:
Ok, so i think the idea in general is interesting. I dont relish the idea of extra signing on critical path, that's not great when its time critical, esp if we're per channel so 64 signatures potentially.

I think realistically this is 'most effective' against a single BN where there's a single source of truth, that's my understanding of the original source of truth.

To me you're talking about getting benefit once you're maybe up to 3 attestations in a slot on a single channel, as with 2 you're giving extra data anyway so its probably marginal / no benefit (this is an assumption im making anyway)

author: .paulharris
content:
To try to articulate my concern better given the above i did a little table....

I used the term probabilistic but its probably wrong, sorry math nerds... basically its just slots * subnets * attesters to get compression. this could be an incorrect assertion for sure.

So by that anyway, it did show my concern if i didnt make mistakes - becasue to get to that kinda of numbers to get an advantage we'd be talking about over 6000 validators on a node, and I think that would be very rare...

Changing subnet count becomes more interesting, or somehow allowing any subnet to be involved, which technically is fine now that we've got no subnet in signing - then really you just need 3 validators in a slot attesting, which is more like 96 on a node....

author: .paulharris
content: Hopefully this better describes my concerns with getting advantages out of this idea, and I think there's a solution somewhere, but just laying out my understanding to hopefully maybe find out if im incorrect.
